title: Dynamic Inventory — Pointers
description: Replace fixed-size arrays with dynamically allocated arrays using new and delete. Allow the user to specify the inventory capacity at startup. Use pointers to pass array data between functions. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
assert 'new ' in cpp, 'Use the new operator to dynamically allocate arrays'
assert 'delete' in cpp, 'Use delete[] to free dynamically allocated memory'
assert 'delete[]' in cpp or 'delete []' in cpp, 'Use delete[] (not just delete) to free arrays'
assert '*names' in cpp or '* names' in cpp or '*productNames' in cpp, 'Declare a pointer for the names array (e.g., string *names)'
assert '*prices' in cpp or '* prices' in cpp or '*productPrices' in cpp, 'Declare a pointer for the prices array (e.g., double *prices)'
assert 'capacity' in cpp.lower() or 'maxSize' in cpp or 'max_size' in cpp, 'Let the user specify the inventory capacity at runtime'
acceptance_criteria: Asks user for inventory capacity at program start
Dynamically allocates string, double, and int arrays using new
Uses pointers to reference the dynamic arrays
Frees all dynamically allocated memory with delete[] before program ends
All existing functionality (add, view, search, sort, save) still works
hints: Syntax: string *names = new string[capacity]; allocates a dynamic array
You MUST use delete[] names; (not just delete names;) to free an array
Place delete[] calls after the do-while loop, before return 0
Dynamic arrays work just like regular arrays with [] indexing
